stories.headline: Trump extends Hormuz deadline to April 6 as Israel kills IRGC navy chief overseeing blockade
stories.body: President Trump extended his deadline for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz by 10 days to April 6, pausing threatened strikes on Iran's power grid that he said were delayed at Tehran's request. Trump claimed talks were going "very well," though Iran denied any direct negotiations were taking place. Hours earlier, Israel killed Alireza Tangsiri, the commander of the IRGC Navy who had overseen the near-total blockade of the strait since the war began on February 28. Israeli defence minister Israel Katz said Tangsiri was "directly responsible" for mining and blocking the waterway, while US Central Command said his death "makes the region safer" and that the IRGC navy was on an "irreversible decline." The head of the IRGC Navy's intelligence directorate, Behnam Rezaei, was also killed in the strike on Bandar Abbas. Meanwhile, Iran's parliament moved to formalize its chokehold on Hormuz by introducing a toll regime for ships transiting the strait.

stories.headline: Wall Street has worst day since Iran war began as Nasdaq enters correction territory
stories.body: US stock markets suffered their steepest decline since the war with Iran began, with the Nasdaq Composite sinking 2.4 percent to enter correction territory, more than 10 percent below its all-time high. The S&P 500 fell 1.7 percent and the Dow dropped 469 points as hopes for a swift resolution to the war dimmed after Iran rejected the US ceasefire proposal and moved to formalize control of the Strait of Hormuz. Brent crude surged 4.8 percent to settle at 101.89 dollars per barrel. Meta Platforms fell 8.2 percent and Alphabet dropped 3.6 percent after the social media addiction verdict earlier in the week. European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde warned about rising inflation and potential interest rate increases, adding to the gloomy mood.

stories.headline: IOC bans transgender women athletes from female Olympic events ahead of 2028 LA Games
stories.body: The International Olympic Committee voted to exclude transgender women from women's events at the Olympics, adopting a new eligibility policy requiring all competitors in female categories to be biological females, as determined by a mandatory gene test. IOC President Kirsty Coventry, the first woman to lead the organization, said even the smallest margins can determine victory and that it would not be fair for biological males to compete in the female category. The policy aligns with President Trump's executive order on sports and will take effect from the Los Angeles Games in July 2028. It also restricts female athletes with differences in sex development, such as two-time Olympic champion Caster Semenya. Coventry said the decision was not driven by external pressure, noting she had made it a priority before Trump's second term.

stories.headline: Judge blocks Pentagon's attempt to 'cripple' Anthropic in first round of AI standoff
stories.body: A federal judge in California sided with AI company Anthropic, blocking directives from President Trump and Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth that ordered all government agencies to immediately stop using Anthropic's tools. Judge Rita Lin wrote that the government was attempting to "cripple Anthropic" and "chill public debate" in what she called "classic First Amendment retaliation." The dispute began after Anthropic refused new contract terms that would have allowed the Pentagon to use its AI tools for "any lawful use," with the company's CEO Dario Amodei concerned this could enable mass surveillance and fully autonomous weapons. Hegseth had labelled Anthropic a "supply chain risk," the first time a US company has received such a designation, normally reserved for firms in adversarial countries. The judge noted that Trump and Hegseth had publicly called the company "woke" and its staff "left-wing nut jobs."

stories.headline: French court sentences Islamic scholar Tariq Ramadan to 18 years for rape
stories.body: A French court sentenced Tariq Ramadan, the 63-year-old Swiss-born Islamic scholar and former Oxford professor, to 18 years in prison for rape. Ramadan denied the charges but his lawyers said he was too ill to attend the trial. The case, which involved multiple complainants, had drawn international attention and sparked debate about abuse of power in academic and religious circles. Ramadan had been one of the most prominent Muslim intellectuals in Europe before the allegations surfaced.

stories.headline: Trump's signature to appear on US banknotes in unprecedented move for a sitting president
stories.body: The US Treasury announced that President Trump's signature will appear on new paper currency alongside Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, making Trump the first sitting president to have his name on US banknotes. Bessent said there was "no more powerful way to recognize the historic achievements of our great country and President Donald J Trump" than dollar bills bearing his name, tying the move to America's 250th anniversary. The first hundred-dollar bills with the new signatures will be printed in June. Since 1861, banknotes have traditionally carried the signatures of the treasurer and treasury secretary, not the president. California Governor Gavin Newsom responded that "Americans will know exactly who to blame as they're paying more for groceries, gas, rent, and health care."

stories.headline: DHS funding lapse becomes longest partial shutdown in US history as Trump promises to pay TSA
stories.body: The Department of Homeland Security funding lapse became the longest partial government shutdown in American history as Congress remained deadlocked, with the Senate failing for a seventh time to advance a bill to partially fund the department. Thousands of TSA agents have been working without pay, causing mounting delays at airports across the country. Trump said he would sign an emergency order to pay TSA agents, though details were not immediately available. The crisis has compounded travel disruptions, with ICE agents now checking identification at security checkpoints in several major airports including JFK, Atlanta, and Houston. In New Jersey, the governor signed a law limiting face coverings for law enforcement including ICE agents during operations.

stories.headline: Kennedy's vaccine agenda hits roadblocks as top jobs remain unfilled and courts block changes
stories.body: Robert F. Kennedy Jr.'s vaccine skepticism is creating growing challenges for the Trump administration, with key positions at federal health agencies remaining unfilled and a court blocking his proposed changes to the childhood vaccine schedule. The setbacks are diminishing Kennedy's influence within the administration at a time when his Health and Human Services Department is under intense scrutiny. The roadblocks come from both the judiciary and the federal bureaucracy, suggesting the limits of executive power in reshaping public health policy.

stories.headline: Helium shortage threatens chip industry as a third of global supply goes offline due to Iran war
stories.body: The war in Iran has taken a third of the world's helium supply offline, creating an invisible bottleneck that threatens the semiconductor industry at a critical moment for AI development. Gas companies are scrambling to reassure chip makers that there will be no disruptions, but the concentration of helium production in conflict-affected regions has exposed a vulnerability in the supply chain that few had anticipated. Helium is essential for cooling and manufacturing processes in chip fabrication, and sustained shortages could slow the production of the advanced processors powering the AI boom.

stories.headline: Elon Musk's X advertising boycott lawsuit dismissed by federal judge
stories.body: A US federal judge dismissed Elon Musk's lawsuit alleging that major advertisers conspired to boycott X, the social media platform formerly known as Twitter. US District Judge Jane Boyle said the company had failed to show it had suffered any harm under federal competition laws. The ruling is a setback for Musk, who had accused companies including Unilever, Mars, and CVS of colluding through the Global Alliance for Responsible Media to withhold advertising revenue. Many major brands had paused spending on the platform after Musk's acquisition in 2022 amid concerns about content moderation changes.

stories.headline: Congress passes anticrisis decree as Sanchez reshuffles cabinet, replacing Montero with Cuerpo and España
stories.body: The Spanish Congress approved the government's five-billion-euro anticrisis decree, with Junts voting in favour and the PP abstaining, securing passage of the 80-measure package to cushion the economic impact of the Iran war including VAT cuts on fuel, electricity, and gas to 10 percent. The same day, Sanchez announced a cabinet reshuffle to replace departing vice-president Maria Jesus Montero, who is leaving to run as the PSOE candidate in the Andalusian elections on May 17. Economy Minister Carlos Cuerpo, 45, an economist with a London School of Economics master's degree, will become first vice-president and economic czar. Arcadi España, a former Valencian regional finance minister, takes over the Finance portfolio. Sanchez described Montero as "the best politician" he has known and "a fundamental pillar" of his eight years in government.

stories.headline: Noelia Castillo, 25, dies by euthanasia in Barcelona after 18-month legal battle with her father
stories.body: Noelia Castillo, a 25-year-old Barcelona woman left paraplegic after a suicide attempt in 2022, died by euthanasia on Thursday following an 18-month legal battle with her father who had tried to block the procedure. The Catalan government approved her request in 2024, but her father, backed by the ultra-conservative group Christian Lawyers, launched successive legal challenges arguing her psychiatric conditions affected her judgement. Courts at every level upheld her autonomy, with the European Court of Human Rights dismissing her father's final appeal on Monday. In a television interview aired hours before her death, Castillo said she had always felt alone, recounted a difficult childhood in care homes and sexual assaults, and said she just wanted to "leave in peace and stop the pain." Her case was the first in Spain where a court had to adjudicate the right to euthanasia.

stories.headline: Carolina Marín announces retirement from badminton, ending storied career at 32
stories.body: Carolina Marín, Spain's Olympic badminton champion, announced her retirement at 32, nearly two years after tearing her knee ligaments during the Paris 2024 semi-finals in an emotional scene seen around the world. The Huelva-born player, who won gold at Rio 2016, three world championships, and seven European titles, said she did not want to put her body at risk any further after suffering three devastating knee injuries since 2019. She had hoped to compete at the European Championships in Huelva in April but acknowledged her body could not cope. Marín said she would still attend the tournament in her hometown to close the circle and return the affection she had received throughout her career. She was awarded the Princess of Asturias Prize for sport in 2024.

stories.headline: Puig shares surge 12 percent as merger talks with Estée Lauder accelerate
stories.body: Barcelona-based fashion and fragrance group Puig saw its shares rise 12.3 percent since confirming merger negotiations with American cosmetics giant Estée Lauder on Monday. The two family-controlled companies are accelerating contacts to try to close the deal as soon as possible, according to market sources. Analysts expect the operation to take the form of a friendly takeover by Estée Lauder in cash and shares with a significant premium, potentially leaving the Puig family with a 20 to 25 percent stake in the combined entity. Estée Lauder reported revenues of 12.3 billion euros last year with a market capitalisation below 30 billion euros, while Puig recorded sales of 5 billion euros in 2025 and is valued above 10 billion euros.

stories.headline: OECD forecasts global inflation rising to 4 percent as Iran war reshapes economic outlook
stories.body: The OECD published its semi-annual report forecasting global inflation rising to 4 percent driven by surging energy prices from the Iran war, warning of broad economic consequences across developed economies. The organization estimated US growth would increase by three tenths this year as its war economy boosts certain sectors, while European economies face the brunt of energy price increases. The report adds to the IMF's earlier downward revisions for Spain and other countries, painting an increasingly challenging picture for the global economy as the conflict enters its second month with no end in sight.

stories.headline: Barcelona launches 23-million-euro crisis package to shield businesses and families from Iran war fallout
stories.body: Mayor Jaume Collboni unveiled a 23-million-euro municipal aid package to mitigate the economic effects of the Iran war, calling it "an illegal and unnecessary war that has plunged the world into unacceptable uncertainty." The plan, agreed with unions and business groups at a Social Dialogue Table, includes four million euros in additional soft loans for small businesses and the self-employed through the B-Crèdits programme, aiming to mobilise 16 million euros total, plus two million to bolster Barcelona Activa's advisory services. Separately, 4.8 million euros will be used to attract investment, new trade fairs, and airline routes as the city positions itself as a "safe and peaceful" alternative to destinations near the conflict zone. Union leaders urged employers not to use the crisis to justify layoffs or price hikes.

stories.headline: Ground handling strikes threaten Easter travel chaos at Barcelona-El Prat airport
stories.body: Ground handling workers at Barcelona-El Prat airport are set to begin strike action this weekend, with 24-hour stoppages called by the UGT union for Saturday and Sunday affecting employees of the Menzies Group, which serves six airlines at the airport. A separate indefinite strike by Groundforce workers began Friday with partial stoppages on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays. Further Menzies strikes are planned from April 2 to 6, raising the prospect of sustained disruption during the busiest Easter travel period. The strikes are the result of failed mediation over working conditions and staff rights, and could cause delays in check-in, boarding, and baggage handling affecting thousands of passengers. The Transport Ministry has not yet established minimum service levels. Aena recommended passengers verify their flight status before heading to the airport.

stories.headline: Thirty Rodalies train drivers caught falsifying medical documents to skip work
stories.body: At least 30 commuter rail drivers in Barcelona's troubled Rodalies network were found to have used falsified medical certificates to obtain paid leave days, according to an investigation by 3CatInfo and La Vanguardia. The workers had used a real certificate from one driver as a template, modifying it to claim four rest days between shifts without losing holidays or salary. The scheme was uncovered after some employees submitted documents in editable formats, while others presented PDFs with identical stamp positions. Metadata analysis confirmed the original document belonged to another driver. Renfe detected the fraud in August 2025 but the investigation reportedly went no further to avoid tensions with the Semaf drivers' union during the delicate transfer of the service to the Catalan government. No new cases have been detected since the matter reached senior management.

stories.headline: Education unions and Catalan government deadlocked after post-strike meeting yields no progress
stories.body: A meeting between Catalan education unions and the regional government produced no agreement following last week's massive teachers' strike, with the Education Department reiterating that it will not renegotiate the labour deal that triggered the walkouts. The USTEC union, which led last Friday's strike claiming 100,000 participants in Barcelona, warned that further industrial action would follow unless the government agrees to sit down and properly negotiate. The stalemate comes after teachers demanded the renegotiation of an agreement they say was struck behind their backs with the CCOO and UGT unions, bypassing the sector's majority unions.

stories.headline: Sagrada Família nears deal to rehouse residents affected by planned Glory Facade stairway
stories.body: The Sagrada Família foundation is finalising an agreement to rehouse the residents of buildings that must be demolished to make way for the monumental stairway planned for the Glory Facade, the basilica's main entrance. The deal would resolve one of the longest-running urban conflicts in Barcelona, where residents of Carrer de Mallorca have lived for decades knowing their buildings stood in the path of Antoni Gaudí's original plans. Separately, a decision on which artist will sculpt the Glory Facade's sculptures is expected this summer, marking the next major artistic milestone as the basilica approaches completion.
